subject: mathematics

sections.title: Introduction
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to identify terms, variables, and coefficients of given algebraic expressions and compare expressions across examples for similarities and differences.

sections.title: Linear Polynomials
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to compute values generated by a linear polynomial (perimeter, payment amount) and to work backward from a given output value to find the corresponding input.

sections.title: Exploring linear patterns
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to predict and extend a growing numeric pattern, evaluate a linear expression for large stage numbers, and work backward from a given term value to its stage number, and to reverse-solve real-world linear scenarios (spending, fare) for an input given an output.

sections.title: Linear growth and linear decay
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to extend a linear growth/decay scenario to evaluate the function at a new input value.

sections.title: Linear Relationships
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to interpret the real-world meaning of the slope and intercept constants obtained in a linear relationship derived from two data points.

sections.title: Visualising linear relationships
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to identify additional points on a given line, compare graphs of related linear equations as the slope or intercept varies, and distinguish the graphs of lines with opposite-sign slopes.

prose_summary: Section 2.1 introduces algebraic expressions through everyday scenarios (cost of pens and pencils, fencing and seeding a garden, area of a wire bent into a rectangle), naming terms, variables, coefficients, and constants, then defines univariate (one-variable) polynomials, introduces degree, and names cubic, quadratic, linear, and constant polynomials by degree, closing with a short exercise set on identifying degrees and coefficients. Section 2.2 defines and explains linear polynomials through examples of perimeter and a chess-club fee structure, establishes that successive integer values of a linear polynomial differ by a constant amount (a linear pattern), introduces linear equations by equating a linear polynomial to a constant and solving for the unknown, and represents a linear polynomial as an input-output function, distinguishing it from a quadratic function; the section closes with an exercise set evaluating linear and quadratic polynomials and solving word problems via linear equations. Section 2.3 explores and generalises a growing geometric pattern of square tiles into the linear expression 2n - 1, defines a linear pattern as a sequence with constant difference between consecutive terms, and extends this idea through pocket-money and auto-rickshaw fare scenarios, deriving and solving linear expressions for the nth term in each case; the section closes with an exercise set requiring students to find linear expressions modelling savings, group size, area, volume, and book-reading scenarios. Section 2.4 explains and distinguishes linear growth and linear decay using a cost-versus-distance function and a water-height-versus-time function, defining each as a constant increase or decrease over equal intervals; the closing exercise set asks students to model plant growth, phone depreciation, village population growth, and a depleting prepaid balance as linear growth or decay expressions. Section 2.5 defines a linear relationship between two variables as y = ax + b and derives the values of a and b from two data points by solving simultaneous equations, illustrated through a telecom data-billing example; the exercise set requires solving for a and b in module-billing, court-rental, and Celsius-Fahrenheit conversion scenarios. Section 2.6 explains and constructs the graph of a linear equation by plotting two points and drawing a line through them, then represents and compares multiple linear equations on shared axes to justify that a represents the slope and b represents the y-intercept, that varying a while holding b fixed changes steepness, that varying b while holding a fixed produces parallel lines, and that the sign of a determines positive (growth) or negative (decay) slope; the closing exercise set asks students to draw and compare several families of lines to consolidate the role of a and b.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: Pens-and-pencils scenario introducing terms, variables, coefficients, and constant in the expression 4x + 5y + 3.
Rectangular garden fencing and seeding cost scenario producing the two-variable expression 200l + 160w + 50lw.
Wire bent into a rectangle; area expressed as the one-variable expression x(10-x) = 10x - x^2.
Perimeter of a square of side x as the linear polynomial 4x.
Chess club joining fee plus per-match charge modelled as the linear polynomial 200 + 50m.
Sum-of-two-numbers word problem solved via the linear equation 2x + 10 = 64; also introduces evaluating a linear expression as an input-output process.
Bela's pocket money depletion modelled as 100 - 5n, solved for the day the amount left equals 40.
Auto-rickshaw fare with flat initial charge plus per-km rate, modelled as 15n - 5 for n >= 2.
Journey cost function C(d) = 100 + 60d illustrating linear growth via a table of values.
Water height in a tank h(t) = 3 - 0.5t illustrating linear decay via a table of values.
Telecom data-billing scenario; finds a and b in y = ax + b from two given (x, y) pairs by solving simultaneous equations.
Plots five points and identifies the line y = 3x by observing the constant ratio between coordinates.
Plots five points and identifies the line through them (negative slope case).
Graphs y = (1/2)x, y = x, y = 2x together to compare slope steepness as a varies for a > 0.
Graphs y = -(1/3)x, y = -x, y = -3x together to compare slope steepness as a varies for negative slopes.
Graphs y = 2x-1, y = 2x+1, y = 2x+5 individually and together, and y = x+3, y = 2x+5, y = 3x-2 together, to establish slope and y-intercept and the parallel-lines property.

enumerated_exercises.description: Find the degrees of four given polynomials.
Write polynomials of degrees 1, 2, and 3.
Find the coefficients of x^2 and x^3 in a given degree-4 polynomial.
Find the coefficient of z in a given cubic polynomial.
Find the constant term of a given cubic polynomial.
Evaluate the linear polynomial 5x-3 at three given values of x (sub-parts i-iii).
Evaluate the quadratic polynomial 7s^2-4s+6 at three given values of s (sub-parts i-iii).
Find present ages of Salil and his mother from a linear-equation age relationship.
Find two positive integers given their difference and ratio.
Find the number of two-rupee and five-rupee coins Ruby has given a total amount and a ratio.
Find the lengths of two pieces of a cut fence given a total length and a ratio.
Find the dimensions of a rectangle given its perimeter and a relation between length and width.
Find a linear expression for a savings account balance growing by a fixed monthly amount.
Find a linear expression for rally membership decreasing by a fixed amount each hour.
Find rectangle areas for given breadths and the linear pattern representing area at fixed length.
Find rectangular box volumes for given heights and the linear pattern representing volume at fixed length and breadth.
Express remaining pages of a book read at a constant daily rate as a linear pattern.
Model plant height growing at a constant monthly rate; find height after 7 months, tabulate, and justify linear growth (sub-parts i-iii).
Model a depreciating mobile phone value; find value after 3 years, tabulate, and justify linear decay (sub-parts i-iii).
Model a growing village population; find population after 6 years, tabulate, and justify linear growth (sub-parts i-iii).
Model a depleting prepaid telecom balance; write the equation, find when balance runs out, and tabulate (sub-parts i-iii).
Find a and b in a learning-platform billing relation y = ax+b from two given module-count/bill pairs.
Find a and b in a gym court-rental billing relation y = ax+b from two given hour/bill pairs.
Find a and b in the Celsius-Fahrenheit linear relation from the freezing and boiling reference points.
Draw five sets of lines (sub-parts i-v) and reflect on the role of slope a and y-intercept b in each set.
Write a degree-3 polynomial in x with a specified coefficient of the x^2 term.
Evaluate two given polynomials at specified values of the variable (sub-parts i-ii).
Find a number from a linear equation describing multiplying and adding fractions.
Find two numbers given one is 5 times the other and a relation after adding 21 to both.
Express savings growing at a fixed monthly rate as a linear pattern and evaluate after 6 months and 2 years.
